title: Opening File with fstream
ques: Which header file is required for file handling in C++? Write a program to create a file "test.txt" and write "Hello" to it.
explanation: `<fstream>` includes `ifstream` (read), `ofstream` (write), and `fstream` (both). `ofstream` creates a file for writing.

title: Reading from File
ques: Use `ifstream` to read the string from "test.txt" created in the previous step and print it to the console.
explanation: `ifstream` opens a file for reading. `getline(stream, string)` reads a full line of text.

title: File Modes
ques: How do you open a file for **appending** data instead of overwriting? Show the syntax.
explanation: `ios::app` is the append mode flag. It ensures data is added to the end of the file.

title: Check if File Exists
ques: Write a snippet using `ifstream` to check if a file named "config.txt" exists.
explanation: If `ifstream` successfully opens the file, `good()` (or implicit boolean conversion) returns true.

title: Binary IO (write)
ques: Use `write()` to save an object of class `Person` (name, age) to a binary file "person.dat".
explanation: `write()` takes a `char*` pointer and size. We cast the object address `&p` to `char*` for binary writing.

title: Binary IO (read)
ques: Read the `Person` object back from "person.dat" using `read()` and print the age.
explanation: `read()` is the counterpart to `write()`. It reads raw bytes directly into the memory location of the object.

title: File Pointers (seekg/seekp)
ques: Explain the difference between `seekg` and `seekp`. Write a line to move the read pointer to the beginning of the file.
explanation: `seekg` moves the "get" (read) pointer. `seekp` moves the "put" (write) pointer. `ios::beg` refers to the beginning.

title: Reading Whole File
ques: What is an efficient way to read an entire file into a **`std::string`** using `stringstream`?
explanation: This is a common C++ idiom. `rdbuf()` returns the file buffer, which is inserted into `stringstream`, then converted to a string.

title: Error Handling in Files
ques: How do you check for end-of-file (EOF) while reading? Loop character by character until EOF.
explanation: `inFile.get(c)` returns specific stream state. If it hits EOF or error, it evaluates to false, terminating the loop.

title: Close File
ques: Why is it important to **close** a file? Does the destructor of `fstream` close it automatically?

answer.content: Closing flushes buffers and releases system resources (file locks). Yes, `fstream` destructor closes the file automatically when the object goes out of scope, but explicit closing is good for checking errors or releasing early.

explanation: Relying on destructors is safe (RAII), but `close()` allows you to handle errors that might occur during closing (like flush failures).
